title: Thomson Reuters Employee Fired After Raising Concerns About Company's ICE Data Contracts
detail: NPR published an investigation on April 21, 2026 reporting that Billie Little, a nearly two-decade veteran at Thomson Reuters' Eagan, Minnesota office, was fired after she and colleagues raised concerns about the company's contracts to supply data to ICE. As masked ICE agents swarmed Minneapolis during the January 2026 Operation Metro Surge — which resulted in the fatal shooting of Renée Macklin Good and Alex Pretti — Little and coworkers grew alarmed that ICE agents were abusing Thomson Reuters' CLEAR investigative database, which provides names, addresses, Social Security numbers, car registration data, and ethnic background data. Thomson Reuters holds a nearly $5 million contract from May 2025 with ICE for 'license plate reader data to enhance investigations for potential arrest, seizure and forfeiture.' An employee committee sent a letter to management in February 2026 asking for transparency about DHS/ICE oversight; shortly after the effort became public, Little was fired. Thomson Reuters is also facing shareholder pressure: British Columbia's public employees' union, a shareholder, filed a proposal requiring an independent human rights impact evaluation of its products used by law enforcement. The Minnesota Star Tribune had previously reported that Thomson Reuters employs thousands at its Eagan campus near the sites of the Minneapolis enforcement surge.

media.caption: Thomson Reuters employee Billie Little was fired after raising concerns about the company's ICE CLEAR database contracts

title: ICE FY2025 Data: 442,637 Removals Released; Agency Sets 1 Million Deportation Annual Target
detail: Axios reported on April 15 and the Washington Times on April 15-16 that ICE's first full-year deportation data under Trump's second term shows 442,637 total removals in FY2025 — combining approximately 330,000 formal removals and 110,000 voluntary returns. This is the first official ICE data release under Trump 2.0 and represents the highest combined removal figure in the agency's history. Through the first half of FY2026 (October 2025 through April 2026), ICE has carried out 234,236 removals — an average of 1,286 per day. In budget documentation submitted to Congress, ICE has now formally set a target of 1 million deportations per year — a figure the agency said reflects 'expanded operational capacity and ongoing commitment to enforcing immigration laws.' At the current pace, ICE would fall short of the 1 million target for FY2026. An independent analysis through the Deportation Data Project showed ICE is on pace for approximately 470,000 total removals in FY2026 unless enforcement is significantly accelerated.

media.caption: ICE FY2025 data shows 442,637 total removals; agency sets 1 million annual deportation target
